title: Work, Energy and Power
learningObjectives: Calculate work done by a constant force
Use the law of conservation of energy to solve problems
introText: Energy is the capacity to do work, and work is energy transferred by a force. Understanding these concepts — along with conservation of energy — lets you solve problems that would be very hard with kinematics alone.

steps.heading: Work Done by a Force
steps.body:
**Work (W) = F × d × cos θ**

Where:
• F = magnitude of force (N)
• d = displacement (m)
• θ = angle between force and displacement

Work is measured in Joules (J = N·m). Work is positive when force and displacement are in the same direction, negative when opposite, and zero when perpendicular (θ = 90°).

Example: You push a 10 kg box 5 m along a floor with a horizontal force of 40 N.
W = 40 × 5 × cos 0° = 200 J
steps.deepDive: The dot product W = **F** · **d** = Fd cos θ is the mathematical definition of work for constant forces. For variable forces, you must integrate: W = ∫F·ds. This is important at university level but not required at NCEA Level 3.

steps.heading: Kinetic and Potential Energy
steps.body:
**Kinetic Energy (KE) = ½mv²**

Any moving object with mass m and speed v has kinetic energy.

**Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE) = mgh**

An object of mass m at height h above a reference level (g = 9.81 m/s²).

**Work-Energy Theorem:**
Net work done on an object = change in kinetic energy
W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu²

This is incredibly powerful — it converts force/displacement problems directly to energy.
steps.deepDive: Elastic potential energy stored in a spring: EPE = ½kx², where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is the extension from natural length. This appears in SHM topics.

steps.heading: Conservation of Energy
steps.body:
**In a closed system with only conservative forces, total mechanical energy is constant:**

KE + GPE = constant
½mv² + mgh = constant

This means: energy at point A = energy at point B
½mv_A² + mgh_A = ½mv_B² + mgh_B

When friction is present, mechanical energy is **not** conserved — some is converted to thermal energy:
W_friction = ΔKE + ΔGPE

Example: A 2 kg ball is released from h = 5 m. Find its speed at the bottom.
½mv² = mgh → v = √(2gh) = √(2 × 9.81 × 5) ≈ 9.9 m/s

steps.heading: Power
steps.body:
**Power (P) = W/t = Fv**

Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. Measured in Watts (W = J/s).

• P = W/t (work divided by time)
• P = Fv (force × velocity, useful for constant-speed motion)

Example: A motor lifts a 500 kg load 10 m in 20 s.
W = mgh = 500 × 9.81 × 10 = 49050 J
P = W/t = 49050/20 ≈ 2450 W ≈ 2.45 kW

steps.questionText: A 3 kg object moving at 4 m/s has what kinetic energy?
A 5 kg ball is dropped from 8 m. Ignoring air resistance, what is its speed just before hitting the ground? (g = 9.81 m/s²)
steps.explanation: Using conservation: ½mv² = mgh → v = √(2gh) = √(2 × 9.81 × 8) = √156.96 ≈ 12.5 m/s.

questions.questionText: A 1500 W engine moves a car at a constant 30 m/s. What is the driving force?
A 10 kg box slides down a frictionless ramp of height 2 m. What is its speed at the bottom? (g = 9.81 m/s²)
